title: Normal vector

infobox.value: is an [object](/wiki/Mathematical_object "Mathematical object") (e.g. a [line](/wiki/Line_(geometry) "Line (geometry)"), [ray](/wiki/Ray_(geometry) "Ray (geometry)"), or [vector](/wiki/Euclidean_vector "Euclidean vector")) that is [perpendicular](/wiki/Perpendicular "Perpendicular") to a given object. For example, the **normal line** to a [plane curve](/wiki/Plane_curve "Plane curve") at a given [point](/wiki/Point_(geometry) "Point (geometry)") is the infinite straight line perpendicular to the [tangent line](/wiki/Tangent_line "Tangent line") to the curve at the point.
is a [vector](/wiki/Vector_(geometry) "Vector (geometry)") perpendicular to a given object at a particular point.
or **normal direction**. A [curvature vector](/wiki/Curvature_vector "Curvature vector") is a normal vector whose length is the [curvature](/wiki/Curvature "Curvature") of the object.

or **normal space** of a manifold at point ![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a) is the set of vectors which are orthogonal to the [tangent space](/wiki/Tangent_space "Tangent space") at ![{\displaystyle P.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/49f4f085fcd14302f4f7a9bbdf77e816cccb3bc9)

of a normal at a point of interest *Q* (analogous to the [foot of a perpendicular](/wiki/Foot_of_a_perpendicular "Foot of a perpendicular")) can be defined at the point *P* on the surface where the normal vector contains *Q*.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Normal vector](/w/index.php?title=Normal_vector&redirect=no "Normal vector"))

Line or vector perpendicular to a curve or a surface

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Normal_vectors2.svg/250px-Normal_vectors2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Normal_vectors2.svg)

A polygon and its two normal vectors

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Surface_normal_illustration.svg/250px-Surface_normal_illustration.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Surface_normal_illustration.svg)

A normal to a surface at a point is the same as a normal to the tangent plane to the surface at the same point.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Tangent_and_normal_to_a_curve.svg/250px-Tangent_and_normal_to_a_curve.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Tangent_and_normal_to_a_curve.svg)

Tangent and normal to a curve in a [Cartesian coordinate system](/wiki/Cartesian_coordinate_system "Cartesian coordinate system").

In [geometry](/wiki/Geometry "Geometry"), a **normal** is an [object](/wiki/Mathematical_object "Mathematical object") (e.g. a [line](/wiki/Line_(geometry) "Line (geometry)"), [ray](/wiki/Ray_(geometry) "Ray (geometry)"), or [vector](/wiki/Euclidean_vector "Euclidean vector")) that is [perpendicular](/wiki/Perpendicular "Perpendicular") to a given object. For example, the **normal line** to a [plane curve](/wiki/Plane_curve "Plane curve") at a given [point](/wiki/Point_(geometry) "Point (geometry)") is the infinite straight line perpendicular to the [tangent line](/wiki/Tangent_line "Tangent line") to the curve at the point.

A **normal vector** is a [vector](/wiki/Vector_(geometry) "Vector (geometry)") perpendicular to a given object at a particular point.
A normal [vector of length one](/wiki/Unit_vector "Unit vector") is called a **unit normal vector** or **normal direction**. A [curvature vector](/wiki/Curvature_vector "Curvature vector") is a normal vector whose length is the [curvature](/wiki/Curvature "Curvature") of the object.
Multiplying a normal vector by −1 results in the [opposite vector](/wiki/Opposite_vector "Opposite vector"), which may be used for indicating sides (e.g., interior or exterior) or orientation (e.g., clockwise vs. counterclockwise, [right handed vs. left handed](/wiki/Right-hand_rule "Right-hand rule")).

In [three-dimensional space](/wiki/Three-dimensional_space "Three-dimensional space"), a **surface normal**, or simply **normal**, to a [surface](/wiki/Surface_(topology) "Surface (topology)") at point *P* is a vector perpendicular to the [tangent plane](/wiki/Tangent_plane "Tangent plane") of the surface at *P*. The [vector field](/wiki/Vector_field "Vector field") of normal directions to a surface is known as *[Gauss map](/wiki/Gauss_map "Gauss map")*. The word "normal" is also used as an adjective: a line *normal* to a [plane](/wiki/Euclidean_plane "Euclidean plane"), the *normal* component of a [force](/wiki/Force "Force"), etc. The concept of normality generalizes to [orthogonality](/wiki/Orthogonality "Orthogonality") ([right angles](/wiki/Right_angle "Right angle")).

The concept has been generalized to [differentiable manifolds](/wiki/Differentiable_manifold "Differentiable manifold") of arbitrary dimension embedded in a [Euclidean space](/wiki/Euclidean_space "Euclidean space"). The **normal vector space** or **normal space** of a manifold at point ![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a) is the set of vectors which are orthogonal to the [tangent space](/wiki/Tangent_space "Tangent space") at ![{\displaystyle P.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/49f4f085fcd14302f4f7a9bbdf77e816cccb3bc9)
Normal vectors are of special interest in the case of [smooth curves](/wiki/Differential_geometry_of_curves "Differential geometry of curves") and [smooth surfaces](/wiki/Differential_geometry_of_surfaces "Differential geometry of surfaces").

The normal is often used in [3D computer graphics](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics") (notice the singular, as only one normal will be defined) to determine a surface's orientation toward a [light source](/wiki/Light_source "Light source") for [flat shading](/wiki/Flat_shading "Flat shading"), or the orientation of each of the surface's corners ([vertices](/wiki/Vertex_(geometry) "Vertex (geometry)")) to mimic a curved surface with [Phong shading](/wiki/Phong_shading "Phong shading").

The **foot** of a normal at a point of interest *Q* (analogous to the [foot of a perpendicular](/wiki/Foot_of_a_perpendicular "Foot of a perpendicular")) can be defined at the point *P* on the surface where the normal vector contains *Q*.
The *[normal distance](/wiki/Normal_distance "Normal distance")* of a point *Q* to a curve or to a surface is the [Euclidean distance](/wiki/Euclidean_distance "Euclidean distance") between *Q* and its foot *P*.

## Normal to space curves

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normal_(geometry)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Normal to space curves")]

Main article: [Frenet–Serret formulas](/wiki/Frenet%E2%80%93Serret_formulas "Frenet–Serret formulas")

Further information: [Curvature vector](/wiki/Curvature_vector "Curvature vector")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Loodlijn_kromme.svg/250px-Loodlijn_kromme.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Loodlijn_kromme.svg)

Normal direction (in red) to a curve (in black).

The normal direction to a [space curve](/wiki/Space_curve "Space curve") is:

:   ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {N} =R{\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {T} }{\mathrm {d} s}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/269feaad3ca5654832e86b5aa3e166df4db7b8f3)

where ![{\displaystyle R=\kappa ^{-1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/be87895b21c20ab397dc51a1eb7000cd859f9e86) is the [radius of curvature](/wiki/Radius_of_curvature "Radius of curvature") (reciprocal [curvature](/wiki/Curvature "Curvature"));
![{\displaystyle \mathbf {T} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9593e3b995a1b57c078873a5ea186c7012e1a5ee) is the [tangent vector](/wiki/Tangent_vector "Tangent vector"),
in terms of the curve position ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {r} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/eca0f46511c4c986c48b254073732c0bd98ae0c1) and arc-length ![{\displaystyle s}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/01d131dfd7673938b947072a13a9744fe997e632):

:   ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {T} ={\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r} }{\mathrm {d} s}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e455d812498cc233af725e50c653e121e255c2f3)

## Normal to planes and polygons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normal_(geometry)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Normal to planes and polygons")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Plane_equation_qtl3.svg/250px-Plane_equation_qtl3.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Plane_equation_qtl3.svg)

Plane equation in normal form

For a [convex](/wiki/Convex_set "Convex set") [polygon](/wiki/Polygon "Polygon") (such as a [triangle](/wiki/Triangle "Triangle")), a surface normal can be calculated as the vector [cross product](/wiki/Cross_product "Cross product") of two (non-parallel) edges of the polygon.

For a [plane](/wiki/Plane_(mathematics) "Plane (mathematics)") given by the general form [plane equation](/wiki/Plane_equation "Plane equation") ![{\displaystyle ax+by+cz+d=0,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5e85b2d4c03909f8388d6424de28d27870977972) the vector ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {n} =(a,b,c)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/047a3d0e2bfbb1489bd35b5893f621af81df22b0) is a normal.

For a plane whose equation is given in parametric form
![{\displaystyle \mathbf {r} (s,t)=\mathbf {r} _{0}+s\mathbf {p} +t\mathbf {q} ,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5caa1e698d576fe77989dbc53ae7dd82fccddb3c)
where ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {r} _{0}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/dbe39f0fedae3334af5c4ffaedf25c9778363400) is a point on the plane and ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {p} ,\mathbf {q} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6a23abdc463ddf71b1b7058a3762b8cda6ff8fb3) are non-parallel vectors pointing along the plane, a normal to the plane is a vector normal to both ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {p} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/dd73e3862cb92b016721b8c492eadb4e8a577527) and ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {q} ,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a4f5466a52a2cbe2cb31294139a2569d83eaa08d) which can be found as the [cross product](/wiki/Cross_product "Cross product") ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {n} =\mathbf {p} \times \mathbf {q} .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c51e6cd9edf1aa628d2c3678583441101df70686)

## Normal to general surfaces in 3D space

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normal_(geometry)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Normal to general surfaces in 3D space")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Normal_vectors_on_a_curved_surface.svg/500px-Normal_vectors_on_a_curved_surface.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Normal_vectors_on_a_curved_surface.svg)

A curved surface showing the unit normal vectors (blue arrows) to the surface

If a (possibly non-flat) surface ![{\displaystyle S}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4611d85173cd3b508e67077d4a1252c9c05abca2) in 3D space ![{\displaystyle \mathbb {R} ^{3}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f936ddf584f8f3dd2a0ed08917001b7a404c10b5) is [parameterized](/wiki/Coordinate_system "Coordinate system") by a system of [curvilinear coordinates](/wiki/Curvilinear_coordinates "Curvilinear coordinates") ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {r} (s,t)=(x(s,t),y(s,t),z(s,t)),}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f2172cb5b4fc6c6baa1abd025bd0360eed0d0889) with ![{\displaystyle s}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/01d131dfd7673938b947072a13a9744fe997e632) and ![{\displaystyle t}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/65658b7b223af9e1acc877d848888ecdb4466560) [real](/wiki/Real_number "Real number") variables, then a normal to *S* is by definition a normal to a tangent plane, given by the cross product of the [partial derivatives](/wiki/Partial_derivative "Partial derivative")
![{\displaystyle \mathbf {n} ={\frac {\partial \mathbf {r} }{\partial s}}\times {\frac {\partial \mathbf {r} }{\partial t}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ddaa2aae8df40ad6085f117068fa0f7d6f26f770)

If a surface ![{\displaystyle S}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4611d85173cd3b508e67077d4a1252c9c05abca2) is given [implicitly](/wiki/Implicit_function "Implicit function") as the set of points ![{\displaystyle (x,y,z)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/22a8c93372e8f8b6e24d523bd5545aed3430baf4) satisfying ![{\displaystyle F(x,y,z)=0,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/51dd0fd6845d9439591f64ff063527a58a5f0487) then a normal at a point ![{\displaystyle (x,y,z)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/22a8c93372e8f8b6e24d523bd5545aed3430baf4) on the surface is given by the [gradient](/wiki/Gradient "Gradient")
![{\displaystyle \mathbf {n} =\nabla F(x,y,z).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/448e560770e02dab889cc242bf9f910d36ff6b3d)
since [the gradient at any point is perpendicular to the level set](/wiki/Level_set#Level_sets_versus_the_gradient "Level set") of ![{\displaystyle S.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/23bbb1f0f6ebdfa78b4fed06049640f7386bb44b)

For a surface ![{\displaystyle S}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4611d85173cd3b508e67077d4a1252c9c05abca2) in ![{\displaystyle \mathbb {R} ^{3}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f936ddf584f8f3dd2a0ed08917001b7a404c10b5) given as the graph of a function ![{\displaystyle z=f(x,y),}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1269bfd7a8dbf5a109363ce2a7992efdf8e406a9) an upward-pointing normal can be found either from the parametrization ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {r} (x,y)=(x,y,f(x,y)),}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/30c6dacf41660068cbb8746141574184d5396652) giving
![{\displaystyle \mathbf {n} ={\frac {\partial \mathbf {r} }{\partial x}}\times {\frac {\partial \mathbf {r} }{\partial y}}=\left(1,0,{\tfrac {\partial f}{\partial x}}\right)\times \left(0,1,{\tfrac {\partial f}{\partial y}}\right)=\left(-{\tfrac {\partial f}{\partial x}},-{\tfrac {\partial f}{\partial y}},1\right);}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6e7a9ea0f1aed9c3b5e1f2eb6c7b9366c65d2d98)
or more simply from its implicit form ![{\displaystyle F(x,y,z)=z-f(x,y)=0,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5e742c57736d0a009746b70ba0536ba8d0bc5ecb) giving ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {n} =\nabla F(x,y,z)=\left(-{\tfrac {\partial f}{\partial x}},-{\tfrac {\partial f}{\partial y}},1\right).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a94ec76d612889e94ca7e181f805c0390879c3ef)
Since a surface does not have a tangent plane at a [singular point](/wiki/Singularity_(mathematics) "Singularity (mathematics)"), it has no well-defined normal at that point: for example, the vertex of a [cone](/wiki/Cone_(geometry) "Cone (geometry)"). In general, it is possible to define a normal almost everywhere for a surface that is [Lipschitz continuous](/wiki/Lipschitz_continuous "Lipschitz continuous").

### Orientation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normal_(geometry)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Orientation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Surface_normals.svg/330px-Surface_normals.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Surface_normals.svg)

A vector field of normals to a surface

The normal to a (hyper)surface is usually scaled to have [unit length](/wiki/Unit_vector "Unit vector"), but it does not have a unique direction, since its opposite is also a unit normal. For a surface which is the [topological boundary](/wiki/Boundary_(topology) "Boundary (topology)") of a set in three dimensions, one can distinguish between two **normal orientations**, the **inward-pointing normal** and **outer-pointing normal**. For an [oriented surface](/wiki/Orientability "Orientability"), the normal is usually determined by the [right-hand rule](/wiki/Right-hand_rule "Right-hand rule") or its analog in higher dimensions.

If the normal is constructed as the cross product of tangent vectors (as described in the text above), it is a [pseudovector](/wiki/Pseudovector "Pseudovector").

### Transforming normals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normal_(geometry)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Transforming normals")]

in this section we only use the upper ![{\displaystyle 3\times 3}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ddc0d4d6106875f8006be1d898512ca5843bad8e) matrix, as translation is irrelevant to the calculation

When applying a transform to a surface it is often useful to derive normals for the resulting surface from the original normals.

Specifically, given a 3×3 transformation matrix ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {M} ,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/38d39eeedc5ff95e3716415596fd9899bdf60eee) we can determine the matrix ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {W} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/04749f1e87cca59c094da23c79cc64b085b0df12) that transforms a vector ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {n} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4a720c341f39f52fd96028dab83edd34d400be46) perpendicular to the tangent plane ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {t} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/16ff63cc74a931900e79b3caacbae3fa8cc66845) into a vector ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {n} ^{\prime }}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ff165a10ab2dcffcc173f7ca30a49767b66d1335) perpendicular to the transformed tangent plane ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {Mt} ,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/61acc24dac0ba26affde652670ef76bfbc9138d2) by the following logic:

Write **n′** as ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {Wn} .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/986cbd5c237fb3db13d5c3d864035759daa45320) We must find ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {W} .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a23c4b1486482a8ea21de862b7141c9b423938cc)
![{\displaystyle {\begin{alignedat}{5}W\mathbb {n} {\text{ is perpendicular to }}M\mathbb {t} \quad \,&{\text{ if and only if }}\quad 0=(W\mathbb {n} )\cdot (M\mathbb {t} )\\&{\text{ if and only if }}\quad 0=(W\mathbb {n} )^{\mathrm {T} }(M\mathbb {t} )\\&{\text{ if and only if }}\quad 0=\left(\mathbb {n} ^{\mathrm {T} }W^{\mathrm {T} }\right)(M\mathbb {t} )\\&{\text{ if and only if }}\quad 0=\mathbb {n} ^{\mathrm {T} }\left(W^{\mathrm {T} }M\right)\mathbb {t} \\\end{alignedat}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f1c81df10a1620b69e53a92c3922be61a0b1fca1)

Choosing ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {W} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/04749f1e87cca59c094da23c79cc64b085b0df12) such that ![{\displaystyle W^{\mathrm {T} }M=I,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e89e87dac99b47e1b4b50b9951dbe3864be0b15d) or ![{\displaystyle W=(M^{-1})^{\mathrm {T} },}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9674baeb53f2ef978b1148ac3a13d60390621961) will satisfy the above equation, giving a ![{\displaystyle W\mathbb {n} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/65c53eed6135e9c624f559b453e8ec9ac7f3d452) perpendicular to ![{\displaystyle M\mathbb {t} ,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6e1f6951e17d54d7c18c181d391b3e8c872052a4) or an ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {n} ^{\prime }}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ff165a10ab2dcffcc173f7ca30a49767b66d1335) perpendicular to ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {t} ^{\prime },}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6947fbc4201b771a4fd4533c14a750e52bbc3afa) as required.

Therefore, one should use the inverse transpose of the linear transformation when transforming surface normals. The inverse transpose is equal to the original matrix if the matrix is orthonormal, that is, purely rotational with no scaling or shearing.

## Hypersurfaces in *n*-dimensional space

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normal_(geometry)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Hypersurfaces in n-dimensional space")]

For an ![{\displaystyle (n-1)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/df88c6333caaf6471cf277f24b802ff9931b133e)-dimensional [hyperplane](/wiki/Hyperplane "Hyperplane") in [![{\displaystyle n}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a601995d55609f2d9f5e233e36fbe9ea26011b3b)-dimensional space](/wiki/N-dimensional_space "N-dimensional space") ![{\displaystyle \mathbb {R} ^{n}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c510b63578322050121fe966f2e5770bea43308d) given by its parametric representation
![{\displaystyle \mathbf {r} \left(t_{1},\ldots ,t_{n-1}\right)=\mathbf {p} _{0}+t_{1}\mathbf {v} _{1}+\cdots +t_{n-1}\mathbf {v} _{n-1},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2f29cf6feaaeddcf097e0750f7189835f456cc04)
where ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {p} _{0}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ae6eb9d63094845ac7406038dc1ed8803f6ef575) is a point on the hyperplane and ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {v} _{i}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/51747274b58895dd357bb270ba1b5cb71e4fa355) for ![{\displaystyle i=1,\ldots ,n-1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0112fea173691e3ee4caca620f96adcf57ae0894) are linearly independent vectors pointing along the hyperplane, a normal to the hyperplane is any vector ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {n} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4a720c341f39f52fd96028dab83edd34d400be46) in the [null space](/wiki/Null_space "Null space") of the matrix ![{\displaystyle V={\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf {v} _{1}&\cdots &\mathbf {v} _{n-1}\end{bmatrix}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1503e5fb8eb4b562085a42fdd6d649e1bf44d994) meaning ⁠![{\displaystyle V\mathbf {n} =\mathbf {0} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f01341a4613c4d0c2ac2950da4993c81f3cec3c4)⁠. That is, any vector orthogonal to all in-plane vectors is by definition a surface normal. Alternatively, if the hyperplane is defined as the solution set of a single linear equation ⁠![{\displaystyle a_{1}x_{1}+\cdots +a_{n}x_{n}=c}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bb4540a22f46a349cfa193a37e1c6a2fdb6dd501)⁠, then the vector ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {n} =\left(a_{1},\ldots ,a_{n}\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1e0b8907b6496c2fbedf1922c2da373fed964e72) is a normal.

The definition of a normal to a surface in three-dimensional space can be extended to ![{\displaystyle (n-1)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/df88c6333caaf6471cf277f24b802ff9931b133e)-dimensional [hypersurfaces](/wiki/Hypersurface "Hypersurface") in ⁠![{\displaystyle \mathbb {R} ^{n}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c510b63578322050121fe966f2e5770bea43308d)⁠. A hypersurface may be [locally](/wiki/Local_property "Local property") defined implicitly as the set of points ![{\displaystyle (x_{1},x_{2},\ldots ,x_{n})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/47d8b3ec62633086002489cad8df4214e9585880) satisfying an equation ⁠![{\displaystyle F(x_{1},x_{2},\ldots ,x_{n})=0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/dbde5e99d3972b079fdfeba6742d06fbf6374a97)⁠, where ![{\displaystyle F}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/545fd099af8541605f7ee55f08225526be88ce57) is a given [scalar function](/wiki/Scalar_field "Scalar field"). If ![{\displaystyle F}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/545fd099af8541605f7ee55f08225526be88ce57) is [continuously differentiable](/wiki/Continuously_differentiable "Continuously differentiable") then the hypersurface is a [differentiable manifold](/wiki/Differentiable_manifold "Differentiable manifold") in the [neighbourhood](/wiki/Neighbourhood_(mathematics) "Neighbourhood (mathematics)") of the points where the [gradient](/wiki/Gradient "Gradient") is not zero. At these points a normal vector is given by the gradient:
![{\displaystyle \mathbb {n} =\nabla F\left(x_{1},x_{2},\ldots ,x_{n}\right)=\left({\tfrac {\partial F}{\partial x_{1}}},{\tfrac {\partial F}{\partial x_{2}}},\ldots ,{\tfrac {\partial F}{\partial x_{n}}}\right)\,.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2836f448c5e183fd7ff4c2bdd816a8e3a12fc476)

The **normal line** is the one-dimensional subspace with basis ![{\displaystyle \{\mathbf {n} \}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a1cfb306c65b75d9d5a533c1df0c911363736e4b)

A vector that is normal to the space spanned by the linearly independent vectors **v**1, ..., **v***r*−1 and falls within the r-dimensional space spanned by the linearly independent vectors **v**1, ..., **v***r* is given by the r-th column of the matrix Λ = *V*(*V*T*V*)−1, where the matrix *V* = (**v**1, ..., **v***r*) is the juxtaposition of the r column vectors. (Proof: Λ is V times a matrix so each column of Λ is a linear combination of the columns of V. Furthermore, *V*TΛ = *I*, so each column of V other than the last is perpendicular to the last column of Λ.) This formula works even when r is less than the dimension of the Euclidean space n. The formula simplifies to Λ = (*V*T)−1 when *r* = *n*.

## Varieties defined by implicit equations in *n*-dimensional space

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normal_(geometry)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Varieties defined by implicit equations in n-dimensional space")]

A **[differential variety](/wiki/Analytic_variety "Analytic variety")** defined by implicit equations in the ![{\displaystyle n}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a601995d55609f2d9f5e233e36fbe9ea26011b3b)-dimensional space ![{\displaystyle \mathbb {R} ^{n}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c510b63578322050121fe966f2e5770bea43308d) is the set of the common zeros of a finite set of differentiable functions in ![{\displaystyle n}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a601995d55609f2d9f5e233e36fbe9ea26011b3b) variables
![{\displaystyle f_{1}\left(x_{1},\ldots ,x_{n}\right),\ldots ,f_{k}\left(x_{1},\ldots ,x_{n}\right).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9d0ce85dd36aa0483511003fad8dc9cdf59a0929)
The [Jacobian matrix](/wiki/Jacobian_matrix "Jacobian matrix") of the variety is the ![{\displaystyle k\times n}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8e173885ca0f29dd406df781144cd635276c68e1) matrix whose ![{\displaystyle i}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/add78d8608ad86e54951b8c8bd6c8d8416533d20)-th row is the gradient of ![{\displaystyle f_{i}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/efdb7feffe027e8a5affff98170471f67ac77aa3) By the [implicit function theorem](/wiki/Implicit_function_theorem "Implicit function theorem"), the variety is a [manifold](/wiki/Manifold "Manifold") in the neighborhood of a point where the Jacobian matrix has rank ![{\displaystyle k.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bcb6778a29f576eb23da1dbddffb73b2571359ac) At such a point ![{\displaystyle P,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bd35af9d5901e795c83d9f519ac73264e74fa595) the **normal vector space** is the vector space generated by the values at ![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a) of the gradient vectors of the ![{\displaystyle f_{i}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/efdb7feffe027e8a5affff98170471f67ac77aa3)

In other words, a variety is defined as the intersection of ![{\displaystyle k}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c3c9a2c7b599b37105512c5d570edc034056dd40) hypersurfaces, and the normal vector space at a point is the vector space generated by the normal vectors of the hypersurfaces at the point.

The **normal (affine) space** at a point ![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a) of the variety is the [affine subspace](/wiki/Affine_subspace "Affine subspace") passing through ![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a) and generated by the normal vector space at ![{\displaystyle P.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/49f4f085fcd14302f4f7a9bbdf77e816cccb3bc9)

These definitions may be extended *verbatim* to the points where the variety is not a manifold.

### Example

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normal_(geometry)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Example")]

Let *V* be the variety defined in the 3-dimensional space by the equations
![{\displaystyle x\,y=0,\quad z=0.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/79aad191142ac3be688c36de767bfa4941a0da8c)
This variety is the union of the ![{\displaystyle x}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/87f9e315fd7e2ba406057a97300593c4802b53e4)-axis and the ![{\displaystyle y}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b8a6208ec717213d4317e666f1ae872e00620a0d)-axis.

At a point ![{\displaystyle (a,0,0),}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c15831931b8ce05012e962ac347c457270e6cc4a) where ![{\displaystyle a\neq 0,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2068d811f09c9ca1437234192c052786eae4e41f) the rows of the Jacobian matrix are ![{\displaystyle (0,0,1)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/25b35543ef5f96df2e9aea6cced9bdd57dab7e1a) and ![{\displaystyle (0,a,0).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d31ff300662cd6f2b2880ff859174cb229b18ee2) Thus the normal affine space is the plane of equation ![{\displaystyle x=a.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e917ab4f4e8b2fa913a9ec28880e3ee4a5a00765) Similarly, if ![{\displaystyle b\neq 0,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/69238caa691e75f448aae578c1d7d93e8c9840b9) the *[normal plane](/wiki/Normal_plane_(geometry) "Normal plane (geometry)")* at ![{\displaystyle (0,b,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2f0cc4529c22456d00199991960eb7ebf2b0da41) is the plane of equation ![{\displaystyle y=b.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4ef1bd48463dbb0709b15f72779baf4c80f980a2)

At the point ![{\displaystyle (0,0,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/28c6fd55d5621fd95ca93549660fbb355fd9bd22) the rows of the Jacobian matrix are ![{\displaystyle (0,0,1)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/25b35543ef5f96df2e9aea6cced9bdd57dab7e1a) and ![{\displaystyle (0,0,0).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/56b5c983e8388a9ad4a6dab868671f87a98f5bb1) Thus the normal vector space and the normal affine space have dimension 1 and the normal affine space is the ![{\displaystyle z}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bf368e72c009decd9b6686ee84a375632e11de98)-axis.

## Uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normal_(geometry)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Uses")]

* Surface normals are useful in defining [surface integrals](/wiki/Surface_integral "Surface integral") of [vector fields](/wiki/Vector_field "Vector field").
* Surface normals are commonly used in [3D computer graphics](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics") for [lighting](/wiki/Computer_graphics_lighting "Computer graphics lighting") calculations (see [Lambert's cosine law](/wiki/Lambert%27s_cosine_law "Lambert's cosine law")), often adjusted by [normal mapping](/wiki/Normal_mapping "Normal mapping").
* [Render layers](/wiki/Render_layers "Render layers") containing surface normal information may be used in [digital compositing](/wiki/Digital_compositing "Digital compositing") to change the apparent lighting of rendered elements.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* In [computer vision](/wiki/Computer_vision "Computer vision"), the shapes of 3D objects are estimated from surface normals using [photometric stereo](/wiki/Photometric_stereo "Photometric stereo").[[1]](#cite_note-1)
* The normal vector may be obtained as the gradient of the [signed distance function](/wiki/Signed_distance_function "Signed distance function").

## Normal in geometric optics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Normal_(geometry)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Normal in geometric optics")]

Main article: [Specular reflection](/wiki/Specular_reflection "Specular reflection")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Reflection_angles.svg/250px-Reflection_angles.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Reflection_angles.svg)

Diagram of specular reflection

The **normal ray** is the outward-pointing ray [perpendicular](/wiki/Perpendicular "Perpendicular") to the surface of an [optical medium](/wiki/Optical_medium "Optical medium") at a given point.[[2]](#cite_note-2) In [reflection of light](/wiki/Reflection_of_light "Reflection of light"), the [angle of incidence](/wiki/Angle_of_incidence_(optics) "Angle of incidence (optics)") and the [angle of reflection](/wiki/Angle_of_reflection "Angle of reflection") are respectively the angle between the normal and the [incident ray](/wiki/Incident_ray "Incident ray") (on the [plane of incidence](/wiki/Plane_of_incidence "Plane of incidence")) and the angle between the normal and the [reflected ray](/wiki/Reflected_ray "Reflected ray").
